title: Science becomes soap opera in Italy
highlights: Feb. 19, 2004, 11:51 PM EST/Source:a href="http://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-srv/front.htm"  ...    It was a scene from a scientific mania in Italy: the unearthing of the dead and famous. Advances in forensics have  ...    Cangrande, for instance, had enemies. He conquered the towns of Vicenza, Belluno, Feltre, Padua and, just before his death, Treviso, where some suspect he was slipped arsenic while he drank from a fountain. His heirs took extraordinary steps to glorify his memory. They mummified him, a procedure usually reserved for saints  ...    has made of defunct Italians. His identification of the remains of Giotto, an early Renaissance fresco painter, created a sensation a few years ago. Examinations of the body revealed concentrations of arsenic, lead and aluminum, probably from long exposure to paint. The toxic metals didn't seem to shorten his life. He died at about 70. title: Secrets of the Medici crypt
highlights: Now a scientist-turned-sleuth is exhuming their corpses  ...    rumoured at thetime of their deaths. The murder investigation is part of a broader project, led by Fornaciari and the  ...    damaged further by the floods that ransacked Florence in 1966.  Fornaciari's priority will be to establish whether or not Francesco died of malaria. If this is the case, the DNA of the malaria virus would still be present in the skeleton. "In that case, Ferdinando would be acquitted," said Fornaciari. If not, he will search for poison. Arsenic was a popular poison of the time, and its effects echo the symptoms Francesco suffered from. The early effects include confusion and drowsiness and headaches. Later, convulsions occur, followed by vomiting, diarrhoea, blood in the urine, muscle cramp, stomach pain and more convulsions. title: The mysterious death of Francesco I de' Medici and Bianca ...
highlights: Modern analytical techniques have allowed re-evaluation of the cause of death of Francesco I de' Medici and his wife, Bianca Cappello. It now seems that the grand-ducal couple died of acute arsenic poisoning and not malaria as previously  ...    More than two and half centuries later, in 1857, the Grand Duke of Lorena, Leopold II, ordered the Medici family tomb to be opened for identification of the bodies and for final burial in the basement of the Medici Chapel, in San Lorenzo. After reading the reports of Francesco's exhumation, referring to a “fairly well preserved” body, and after examining the portraits drawn by two artists present for the recognition of the body, Féynes hypothesised that Francesco's death could be due to arsenic poisoning.4Féynes was well aware that arsenic may slow down the  ...
